1: ʾÎš tôkaḥôt maqšeh-ʿōrep = “A man who is often reproved (tôkaḥôt, reproofs) yet stiffens his neck (maqšeh-ʿōrep)” — qāšāh ʿōrep (stiffen the neck) is the covenant-violation posture of Deuteronomy 31:27; the neck-stiffening of the wilderness generation. Peta' yiššābēr wĕʾên marpēʾ = “will suddenly be broken beyond remedy.”> Marpēʾ (healing, remedy) — the absence of remedy after the break; the repetition of reproof without response exhausts the window of healing.
2: Birĕbôt ṣaddîqîm yiśmaḥ hāʿām = “When the righteous are in authority, the people rejoice” — the political theology of joy: righteous rule produces communal flourishing that is experienced as joy. Ûbimšōl rāšāʿ yēʾānah ʿām = “when the wicked rule, the people groan.”> ʾĀnaḥ (groan/sigh) — the bodily expression of distress under oppressive governance. The groan of the people under the wicked is the inverse of their song under the righteous.
3: ʾÎš ʾōhēb ḥokmāh yĕśammaḥ ʾābîw = “Whoever loves wisdom brings his father joy” — the parent-child wisdom theme of Proverbs 10:1. Wĕrōʿēh zônôt yĕʾabbēd hôn = “but a companion of prostitutes wastes his wealth (hôn).”> ʾābad (perish/destroy/lose) applied to hôn (wealth/substance) — the seductive woman of Proverbs 7 as an economic as well as spiritual destroyer.
4: Melek bĕmišpāṭ yaʿămîd ʾāreṣ = “A king (melek, H4428, dispute_level=2) establishes the land through justice” — šāphaṭ (justice) as the architectural force of political stability. Wĕ'îš tĕrûmôt yehersennāh = “but one who accepts bribes (tĕrûmôt, tribute-offerings/bribes) tears it down.”> The term tĕrûmāh normally means the priestly offering set apart; its ironic use for bribes suggests that a bribe is a tribute offered to the wrong altar — corrupting what should be holy judgment.
5: Geber meḥallēq ʿal-rēʿēhû = “A man who flatters his neighbor” — ḥālaqqāh (smooth/flattery) — the smooth speech that disarms honest assessment. Rešet pôrēś ʿal-pĕʿāmāyw = “spreads a net for his feet.”> The flatterer's smooth words are a trap laid under the feet of the one being praised; the danger of flattery is that it prevents the flattered person from seeing clearly and so they walk into what they cannot see.
6: Bĕpeša' ʾîš-rāʿ môqēš = “In the transgression of an evil man there is a snare” — the wicked person's sin is itself a trap that catches them. Wĕṣaddîq yārûn wĕyiśmāḥ = “but the righteous sing (rûn) and rejoice.”> Rûn (shout for joy/sing a ringing cry) — the vocal expression of liberation; the righteous person moves through the same territory the wicked stumbles in and sings rather than stumbles.
7: Yōdēaʿ ṣaddîq dîn dallîm = “The righteous man knows (yāddaʿ, H3045) the cause of the poor” — yādaʿ here carries the full weight of “care-about” (as in “the LORD knows the way of the righteous,” Psalms 1:6): not mere intellectual knowledge but engaged concern. Rāšāʿ lō'-yābîn dāʿat = “the wicked man does not care to understand it.”> The refusal to understand is itself a moral act: willful epistemic distance from the poor's situation is wickedness.
8: ʾAnšê lāṣôn yāpîḥû qiryāh = “Scornful men inflame (pûaḥ, blow/breathe) a city” — pûaḥ (blow like wind) applied to city-inflaming: the scornful breathe on the coals of social conflict until a city burns. Waḥăkāmîm yāšîbû ʾāp = “but wise men turn away anger (ʾāp, nose/anger).”> The same nose-image: ʾāp (wrath, lit. the snorting nose) is turned back by wisdom; the hot breath of scorn is countered by the cooling counsel of the wise.
9: ʾÎš ḥākām niśpāṭ ʾet-ʾîš ʾĕwîl = “If a wise man contends in court (šāphaṭ, H8199, dispute_level=2) with a fool” — šāphaṭ carries the range of judge/govern/decide; in the court setting it is “to dispute/plead.”> Wĕrāgaz wĕśāḥaq wĕʾên nāḥat = “the fool only rages and laughs, and there is no quiet.”> The fool's courtroom behavior — alternating between rage and dismissive laughter — makes rational proceedings impossible; the wise man's case cannot be heard.
10: ʾAnšê dāmîm yiśnĕʾû-tām = “Bloodthirsty men hate the upright (tām, blameless/complete)” — the blameless person is the target of those who live by violence; innocence provokes those whose self-justification depends on everyone being as guilty as they are. Wîšārîm yĕbaqqĕšû napšô = “but the righteous seek to protect his life (nepeš, H5315).”> Nepeš here = life/person; the righteous protect the vulnerable blameless person.
11: Kol-rûḥô yôṣîʾ kĕsîl = “A fool vents all his spirit/feelings (rûaḥ, H7307)” — rûaḥ has the range: spirit / breath / wind / disposition / feeling. Here “all his spirit” = every impulse, emotion, and reaction immediately expressed. Wĕḥākām bĕʾāḥôr yĕšabbĕḥennāh = “but a wise man quietly holds it back.”> Šābaḥ (calm/still/hold back) — the same root used in Psalms 89:9 of YHWH stilling the sea: wisdom rules the inner weather the way YHWH rules the storm.
12: Mōšēl maqšîb ʿal-dĕbar-šāqer = “If a ruler listens to lies” — the leader who opens himself to false reports has already compromised the governing function. Kol-mĕšārĕtāyw rĕšāʿîm = “all his officials become wicked.”> The moral ecology of leadership: a ruler who tolerates lying selects for wicked officials who survive by telling him what he will accept. Corruption propagates downward from receptive leadership.
13: Rāš wĕʾîš tĕkākîm nipgāšû = “The poor man and the oppressor have this in common” — the unexpected juxtaposition. Mĕʾîr ʿênê-šĕnêhem YHWH = “the LORD gives light to the eyes of both.”> The divine gift of sight (life-capacity) is democratically given — oppressor and oppressed both receive the same basic endowment from YHWH. This is not moral equivalence but creational equality: YHWH is Creator of both.
14: Melek šôpēṭ bĕʾĕmet dallîm = “A king (melek, H4428) who judges (šāphaṭ, H8199) the poor with faithfulness (ʾĕmet, H571, dispute_level=2)” — three disputed terms converge. ʾĕmet ranges across truth/faithfulness/reliability; here it describes the quality of the king's judgment toward the powerless. Kissĕʾô lāʿad yekkôn = “his throne will be established forever.”> The stability of the throne is directly proportional to the faithfulness of its justice toward the most vulnerable.
15: Šēbeṭ wĕtôkaḥat yittēn ḥokmāh = “The rod and reproof give wisdom” — the dual instruments of formation: šēbeṭ (rod/staff, physical correction) and tôkaḥat (verbal reproof). Wĕnaʿar mĕšullāḥ mēbîš ʾimmô = “but a child left to himself (mĕšullāḥ, sent away/released) brings shame to his mother.”> The released child — uncorrected, unformed — is a community liability; his mother bears the social cost of his formation failure.
16: Birĕbôt rĕšāʿîm yirbeh-pāšaʿ = “When the wicked increase (rābāh), transgression increases” — multiplication of wicked persons generates its own momentum: wickedness begets wickedness in a social amplification effect. Wĕṣaddîqîm bĕmappaltām yirʾû = “but the righteous will see their downfall.”> The righteous are spectators of divine judgment on the wicked; they do not need to avenge themselves.
17: Yassēr binkā wînîḥekā = “Discipline your son and he will give you rest” — yāsar (discipline/chastise) as the formative act that produces peace in the parent. Wĕyittēn maʿadannîm lĕnapšekā = “he will give delight (maʿadannîm, delicacies/luxuries) to your soul (nepeš, H5315).”> Nepeš here = self/inner life; the disciplined son's character is the parent's spiritual luxury — the inner satisfaction deeper than material pleasure.
18: Bĕʾên ḥāzôn yippāraʿ ʿām = “Where there is no prophetic vision (ḥāzôn), the people are unrestrained (pāraʿ)” — ḥāzôn is the technical prophetic term for divine revelation received in a vision (Isaiah 1:1; Obadiah 1:1). The absence of prophetic word dissolves social restraint; without revelation the moral framework loses its authoritative grounding. Wĕšōmēr tôrāh ʾašrêhû = “but blessed is the one who keeps the law.”> The pairing of prophetic vision and tôrāh-keeping as dual sources of order is the governing theme of the verse.
19: Bidbārîm lō'-yiwwāsēr ʿābed = “A servant cannot be corrected by mere words” — the limits of verbal instruction in certain contexts. Kî yābîn wĕʾên maʿăneh = “for he understands but will not respond.”> The servant who hears and understands but refuses to respond illustrates the gap between intellectual reception and behavioral change — understanding without transformed will.
20: Ḥāzîtā ʾîš ʾāṣ bidbārāyw = “Do you see a man who is hasty (ʾūṣ) in his words?”> — ʾūṣ (hasten/urge) — the urgency of words not thought through. Tiqwāh lĕkĕsîl mimmennû = “There is more hope for a fool than for him.”> The highest negative comparison in Proverbs: to be ranked worse than the fool is to be classified as a special case of recklessness. The hasty-tongued person is beyond the fool because the fool at least may be ignorant; the hasty speaker knows but does not pause.
21: Mĕpannēq minnōʿar ʿabdô = “Whoever pampers (pānaḳ) his servant from childhood” — pānaḳ (pamper/indulge/coddle) — the excessive softening of the servant's formation. Wĕʾaḥărîtô yihyeh māndôn = “will find that in the end he has become like a son.”> The indulged servant loses the working relationship and claims familial entitlements; overly soft treatment erodes proper distinctions.
22: ʾÎš-ʾap yĕgāreh mādon = “An angry man (ʾap, nose/anger) stirs up strife” — the nose-anger idiom again (cf. v8): the short-tempered person is a chronic strife-generator. Ûbaʿal ḥēmāh rab-pāšaʿ = “and a hot-tempered man (baʿal ḥēmāh, master of wrath) abounds in transgression.”> The hot-tempered person's transgression multiplies because anger bypasses the moral deliberation that restrains sin.
23: Gaʾăwat ʾādām tašpîlennû = “A man's pride (gaʾăwāh) will bring him low” — gaʾăwāh (pride/arrogance/majesty) — the same root as divine majesty when applied to YHWH (Psalms 93:1), but here human presumption. Ûšĕpal rûaḥ yitmōk kābôd = “but the one who is humble of spirit (šĕpal rûaḥ) will obtain honor (kābôd).”> Both rûaḥ (H7307) and kābôd (H3519, dispute_level=2) are contested: rûaḥ = spirit/breath/disposition; kābôd = honor/glory/weight. The humble-spirited person receives the weighty honor that the proud person grasps after and loses.
24: Ḥôlēq im-gannāb śônēʾ napšô = “Whoever partners with a thief hates himself (śônēʾ napšô)” — the self-destructive logic of complicity: partnering with wickedness is hatred toward one's own life. ʾAlāh yišmaʿ wĕlō' yaggîd = “he hears the curse but does not disclose it.”> The silent witness who hears a public oath-curse (calling for disclosure of wrongdoing) and refuses to testify — both guilty participant and cowardly bystander.
25: Ḥardat ʾādām yittēn môqēš = “The fear of man (ḥardat ʾādām) lays a snare” — human-directed fear as a trap; the anxiety about what people will think or do constrains action and leads into self-protective compromise. Ûbôṭēaḥ baYHWH yĕśuggāb = “but whoever trusts in YHWH (H3068) is kept safe (śāgab, set on high/made inaccessible).”> Śāgab = to be placed on an inaccessible height — beyond the reach of the snare. The trust that transfers fear from humans to YHWH produces the elevation the snare cannot reach.
26: Rabbîm mĕbaqqĕšîm pĕnê-môšēl = “Many seek the ruler's favor (pĕnê-môšēl, the face of the ruler)” — court-seeking as the normal mode of obtaining advantage; bāqaš pānîm = to seek the face = to appeal for access/approval. Ûmêʾet YHWH mišpaṭ-ʾîš = “but justice for a man comes from YHWH.”> The verse redirects the petitioner: the court of ultimate appeal is not the human ruler but YHWH. Human rulers can be circumvented, corrupted, or inaccessible; YHWH's justice is the final and reliable recourse.
27: Tôʿăbat ṣaddîqîm ʾîš ʿāwel = “An unjust man is an abomination to the righteous” — the mutual repulsion of righteousness and wickedness: the tôʿēbāh (abomination) that YHWH feels toward wickedness (Proverbs 12:22; Proverbs 15:8) is felt by the righteous as well — moral revulsion is a feature of righteous character. Wĕtôʿăbat rāšāʿ yĕšar-dārek = “and the one who walks uprightly is an abomination to the wicked.”> The mutual abomination is symmetric: righteousness is intolerable to wickedness as wickedness is to righteousness.
